title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
author: Ben Horowitz

chapters.title: The hard thing is usually overlooked
chapters.text:
There is no recipe to “cook” the best company ever. You must deal with the unknown ingredients and improvise in the process. Embracing this thought will spread your wings. Accept that there are no easy answers, and the fear will stop blocking your actions. No, we are not here to feed you with illusions; you will be scared and sometimes even terrified. But it will not stop you from acting.

Being a CEO is going down an unexplored road full of unpredictable twists. It is challenging because of the emotions versus logic battle and the life-or-death decisions you make. Pressure, fears, people dependent on you, your reputation, and fighting for the company's survival is indeed a struggle nobody taught you to deal with.

Widen your network, seek advice, talk to people, and understand that you are not alone. Care about your people first, then comes the product. People create the product, not vice versa. There will be many problems along the way, and they will only be solved if the people in charge know about them. Increase your strengths by staying focused on the positives; don't berate yourself. Embrace the struggle and find peace in this fantastic way toward your dreams.

Try this

• Get inspired by Jeff Bezos's letter to his shareholders in 1997 explaining Amazon's story.
• Write down what your company is about or will be about: its unique value on the market and the change it brings.
• Ask yourself why people should enjoy working with you and buying from you.

chapters.title: Embrace the struggles to be great
chapters.text:
Many words can characterize Ben Horowitz's business path — a rollercoaster, a survival cycle, an evolution. It's been quite a journey filled with both euphoria and terror. As the young CEO of Loudcloud, Ben Horowitz managed to build the company with the fastest growth back then. He knows how to walk the edge of bankruptcy very well. He felt pressure from having employees depend on him and making hard decisions. Fear of losing other people's money and a ruined reputation followed along the way too. When the company's survival was at stake, it was time to hear fifty “no’s” for the sake of one “yes.” It was a point to choose the right people for advice, think through the worst-case scenarios, and take full responsibility for the company's path.

Being a CEO is like skating on thin ice. Keeping your focus on the risks leaves you with less chance of falling. The business depends on various external factors, not only in your control. It’s incredibly tough to play a survival game every day. Struggles come when things don't go as expected. They arise when you start questioning your vision, abilities, and why you started in the first place. You can smell struggles as you notice your space filled with fear of losing something that has become a part of you. But you are not alone in this.

Your struggle is a trampoline to a more significant result.

There are no magic answers for all problems, but here are some things that might be helpful:

• Don't carry the entire burden yourself. The more people involved in solving the problem, the better. Your focus is to save the company without killing yourself with work.
• You can always make a move toward your business goal. If you feel stuck, you just haven't come up with an idea yet.
• Try to make it until tomorrow. Survive as long as possible; you might see solutions you have yet to notice.
• Refrain from measuring your value as a CEO after making mistakes. Everybody fails. Beating yourself up doesn't help.
• If you want a thriving empire, accept the challenge.

chapters.title: Don't sugarcoat difficult topics
chapters.text:
Being constantly under pressure, Ben Horowitz found failures incredibly difficult. Whenever there was a loss, he stretched the truth. But all employees are in one boat, and the CEO can't be the only one who knows it is sinking. Work is only effective when people realize what they are working toward and which challenges they must overcome.

Avoid throwing white lies at employees instead of telling it like it is.

Surprisingly, Ben Horowitz realized that employees dealt with losses better. For example, he couldn't fix the problems with a product code because he wasn't the one coding. All he could mostly do was worry — but how could it help?

Give the problem to the people who could not only fix it, but who would also be personally excited and motivated to do so. ~ Ben Horowitz

Here are three main reasons to be open about your company's issues:

1. Trust.

It is the basis of building effective communication. Words have no value if people don’t believe them.

2. The more brains are handling the challenges, the better.

Having intelligent people and not putting their bright minds to use must be a business crime.

3. Good news spreads slowly; bad news spreads quickly.

People can only quickly solve a problem if they know about it. A healthy company culture should encourage transparency and courage to share bad news.

Here are some of the right ways to lay people off:

• Don't wait too long to talk about layoffs. Rumors will undermine the employees’ trust in managers and executives.
• A layoff hits the employees staying, too — so prepare to support them.
• As a CEO, you must be honest and transparent in your communication: admit that it's the company's failure and not personal.
• Show people that you care and acknowledge the value of their work.

Analyzing why you hired the wrong person is beneficial whenever you fire an executive. You may have failed to define your needs or didn't evaluate the person's strengths thoroughly.

Another challenge is firing your friend. Get ready to deal with the two emotions your friend will feel: embarrassment and betrayal. Also, sound decisive — if you leave space to change the decision, you will end up with a mess in your friendship and work.

chapters.title: Competent people create powerful companies
chapters.text:
When you tell your workers what to do, ensure they understand why. The strongest motivation is inner motivation. And it comes from your employees having a strong belief and deep understanding of why they should do what you're telling them to do.

Take care of the people, the products, and the profits — in that order. ~ Ben Horowitz

Be in touch with how your people feel about their work. The most effective workers are the ones who like what they are doing and spend their time performing their best rather than worrying about poor work communication or unclear KPIs. It's no secret that the path to building a good company is full of challenges or things that may go wrong. Make sure you hear from your employees about all the wrongs. One way to do it is to take advantage of one-on-ones with managers.

People run your company's every vital process. Their expertise defines the value that the company brings to the market.

The more trained your people are — the more successful your company is.

Companies need to invest in their staff recruitment process. Here are key reasons why:

• Training gives staff leverage in their industry. As you train your people, they implement what they learned. This only means that they'll be more productive.
• Training is the foundation of performance management. How will you justify firing or promoting an employee if your expectations are unclear?
• If you don't spend money on people training, you will spend it on fixing errors with your product. As you hire new engineers and don't invest in training, they will make mistakes and create defective products.
• An outstanding training program is an investment in your employees and your company. Ben Horowitz decided to read exit interviews for Netscape to understand why people quit big tech companies. He found two main reasons:
• Hate for the manager.
• Stagnancy.

When employees realize their manager isn't helping them improve or the company isn't investing in them, they start looking for better options elsewhere.

chapters.title: Hire the best to create the best
chapters.text:
Great companies devote their time and money to recruiting the best talent. But what are the rules that guide hiring processes? Is it okay to recruit a worker from your friend's company? Is an employee more critical than your CEO friend?

Your network is your treasure.

Poaching an employee from your friend's company is an easy way to lose a valuable business relationship and a good friend. Confirm your friend's approval to hire from their company as a solution. However, be aware that it can cause a domino effect of other employees wanting to work for you. Evaluate the impact of this hire beforehand.

Another tricky thing is hiring executives from big companies to small ones. Company size distinguishes the rhythm, skill set, and types of processes. So a professional executive from a big company might not be a good fit for a small one. For example, a person can be great at organizational design but not know about building processes from zero. Ask them if they want to be a manager or a creator. Make a list of specific skills you need, check for huge mismatches in expertise during an interview, and put reasonable effort into onboarding.

Here are some critical pieces of advice on hiring the best person for the job:

• Create a clear picture of what you need. Not knowing your goals and needs is equal to not achieving them. Write down the qualities you seek and the flaws you can compromise with.
• Get more involved with the most important executive roles. Gaining knowledge about them makes you better at hiring and managing them. It can happen by acting in this role or interviewing professionals about it.
• Organize a team that will help you identify the best candidate.
• Gather references.
• Decide by yourself. As a CEO, you are the only person that understands the whole picture, so the responsibility is on you.

Also, your management team and HR organization must ensure adequate compensation, performance management, motivation, beneficial training, and onboarding. To hire a great HR team, concentrate on process designing and communication skills. Besides, HR must genuinely desire to see when things go wrong and be a trusted adviser.

chapters.title: Being a CEO means making difficult decisions
chapters.text:
Ben Horowitz considers emotional well-being and stability the hardest things to manage as a CEO. It is the primary battle you must win to master all the hard skills, like organizational design. Separate your emotions from the problems. And don't be too harsh on yourself; you can learn to be a CEO only by being a CEO.

Concentrate on what you can do well instead of where you failed or might fail.

One of the hard decisions comes when your employee asks you for a promotion or a higher salary. How do you prevent your employees from striving for unjustified promotions or having problems with managers? The first step is identifying their ambition. You need them to desire the company's success; personal goals should be a side product. The main priority is building a company and seeing work through team lenses. The second step is creating a well-organized, thoroughly considered procedure for giving promotions and titles. Remember, pay increases should depend on performance rather than ambition. By making a decision concerning one person, you set an example for the others — make sure you are being fair.

Every tough decision will feel worse to you as a CEO than in reality. You will have a few moments when you think it's over and you are in trouble. Don't get swept away by an emotional wave. Talk to your friends to feel connected and supported, or pour everything out on paper. Also, focus on how you can succeed rather than why you may fail. When Ben Horowitz asked great CEOs how they coped, they answered, “I didn't quit.” Both the hero and the coward have the same feelings; what distinguishes them is their actions.

Making hard decisions in a progressive and succeeding company culture is easier. Company culture consists of distinguishing features of employees' behavior that make you unique in the market. If you want to form a strong company culture, find things that can:

• Create a better work environment.
• Highlight and fulfill your fundamental values.
• Motivate you to improve your product value.

chapters.title: Your vision will make you a leader
chapters.text:
There is an ongoing eternal discussion on what makes a good leader. According to Ben Horowitz, three key things are:

• The power to define and communicate the vision will determine if people stay and work to the best of their abilities. The vision must be exciting enough to make your employees follow you even when the company is going through a rough patch. The source of motivation and belief makes people stay up late and develop creative solutions to challenges. When Apple was a step away from bankruptcy, Steve Jobs's vision helped the employees get back on track.
• The right type of ambition. The main priority of the CEO must be the company. Why would your employees work for the company's sake if you work for only yourself? Your workers must feel that you care about them and the place they dedicate their energy to.
• The capacity to carry out the vision. Competence and courage are the keys to building trust with employees.

Evaluating your performance as a CEO is an essential part of being a great leader.

You can't do well on a test if you don't know the questions beforehand — the same rule works for measuring your CEO performance. Ask yourself these questions to review and improve your work.

• Are you proficient in your responsibilities?
• How fast and effective are your decisions?
• Can you build a successful strategy?

A company leader is also responsible for the company story, which is not about achieving huge KPIs. It is about the value it brings to humanity, the excitement one should feel while applying for a job there, and the eagerness to invest or buy from your company. The main question of your story is why people should feel this way about what you create with your team.

Did you know? The central concept of giving feedback is to wrap it up as a sandwich. Compliment first (this is the first bread slice), deliver the critics (this is the stuffing), and encourage their strengths and value (the second slice of bread).

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
There is no recipe to “cook” the best company ever. You must deal with the unknown ingredients and improvise in the process. Embracing this thought will spread your wings. Accept that there are no easy answers, and the fear will stop blocking your actions. No, we are not here to feed you with illusions; you will be scared and sometimes even terrified. But it will not stop you from acting.
